infons.title: Keywords

passages.text: Effective point spread function modeling of photoacoustic remote sensing microscopy towards resolution enhancement

Photoacoustic remote sensing (PARS) microscopy, as an all-optical non-contact imaging modality, has demonstrated considerable potential in biomedical applications. However, current studies on PARS resolution remain limited to experimental characterization, hindering the systematic analysis and optimization of its imaging performance. In this study, we develop an effective point spread function (PSF) model for PARS microscopy, revealing that the system resolution is jointly determined by the PSFs of both the excitation and probe paths. Utilizing a developed multipath confocal PARS (MC-PARS) system, we evaluate the model through numerical simulations and experimental measurements across various excitation-probe wavelength combinations. The results show excellent agreement between theoretical predictions and experimental data. Furthermore, by introducing a confocal configuration into the probe path, we achieve controlled contraction of the effective PSF, resulting in a twofold enhancement in axial resolution of MC-PARS. This work establishes a reliable framework for predicting and optimizing PARS resolution, providing a foundation for surpassing the resolution limit in non-contact photoacoustic imaging through advanced PSF engineering.
Introduction

Photoacoustic microscopy (PAM) is a functional imaging modality that integrates optical absorption with acoustic detection, demonstrating vast potential in diverse fields such as neuroscience, cancer research, and histopathology. Notably, optical-resolution PAM (OR-PAM) utilizes tight optical focusing to circumvent the acoustic diffraction limit, enabling micron-scale or even sub-micron scale lateral resolution for cellular-level visualization. However, conventional OR-PAM typically relies on liquid coupling to facilitate efficient photoacoustic signal transmission. This dependency limits its application in contact-sensitive environments, such as wound healing, burn assessments, and imaging of fragile biological tissues.

Photoacoustic remote sensing (PARS) microscopy enables non-contact photoacoustic signal detection by employing a continuous-wave probe beam, confocal with the excitation light, to capture transient refractive index changes induced by initial photoacoustic pressure. Its all-optical and completely non-contact design eliminates the need for coupling agents. Consequently, PARS has been successfully applied in diverse biomedical fields, including pathological diagnosis, ocular imaging, and blood oxygen saturation monitoring. However, while the underlying signal generation mechanisms of PARS have been explored, investigations into its resolution from a system-level perspective remain largely confined to experimental measurements. Consequently, the theoretical analysis methods for PARS resolution and the subsequent identification of governing factors have not been developed. Such limitations impede systematic optimization of three-dimensional (3D) imaging performance, thereby hindering the application of PARS microscopy in precision-demanding scenarios, such as visualizing 3D cellular structures, monitoring microangiogenesis, and investigating biological microenvironments.

The point spread function (PSF) serves as the fundamental theoretical tool for analyzing and optimizing the resolution of optical systems. In fluorescence microscopy, active compression or modulation of the system effective PSF has driven the development of various super-resolution imaging techniques, such as fluorescence emission difference (FED), stimulated emission depletion (STED), and structured illumination microscopy (SIM). In contrast to these PSF-engineered fluorescence imaging modalities, PARS microscopy is an absorption-based technique that relies on endogenous optical contrast. Its imaging process deviates from the conventional photon-in, photon-out paradigm and instead involves a multi-stage physical transduction sequence, including optical absorption, initial photoacoustic pressure generation, elasto-optic refractive index modulation, and subsequent optical detection. This sequence fundamentally distinguishes PARS microscopy from conventional optical imaging modalities. However, a formal definition of the effective PSF that describes this unique imaging process remains unestablished. The absence of this crucial theoretical model prevents the quantitative assessment of how key parameters, including excitation and probe wavelengths, affect the spatial resolution in PARS microscopy. Furthermore, it constrains the integration of advanced PSF engineering strategies, such as deconvolution and structured illumination for enhancing PARS resolution.

In this study, we present a detailed theoretical derivation of the PSF model for PARS microscopy, validating its effectiveness through both numerical simulations and experimental measurements to establish a theoretical foundation for PARS resolution enhancement. By analyzing the optical configuration and the underlying physical imaging process, we derived an analytical expression for the effective PSF of PARS microscopy, thereby explicitly linking PARS spatial resolution to the characteristics of the excitation and probe beams. Utilizing this model, we simulated the theoretical resolutions under various excitation-probe wavelength combinations, providing a reliable reference for experimental parameter optimization. Furthermore, we developed a multipath confocal PARS (MC-PARS) system and characterized its actual spatial resolution across different wavelength combinations. The experimental results demonstrate high consistency with our theoretical predictions, confirming the accuracy of the proposed PSF model. Moreover, by integrating a confocal configuration into the detection path of the MC-PARS system, we actively modulated and compressed the PSF by adjusting the pinhole aperture size, ultimately achieving a twofold enhancement in axial resolution. Our work not only offers an effective strategy for improving the spatial resolution of PARS, but also establishes a comprehensive framework that bridges theoretical calculation and experimental optimization. More importantly, it provides a reliable theoretical and practical foundation for implementing advanced PSF engineering strategies in PARS microscopy, paving the way towards surpassing resolution limits in non-contact PAM.
Principle and theory

Simplified schematics of the optical paths for different imaging modalities in PARS microscopy. (a) Backscattering mode (BS-mode) of PARS. (b) Photoacoustic mode (PA-mode) of PARS. PBS, polarizing beam splitter; QWP, quarter-wave plate; PD, photodiode; OL, objective lens; DM, dichroic mirror.

A PARS microscopy comprises a detection path and an excitation path, where both beams are confocally focused onto the same point of the sample through an objective lens (OL). The PARS microscopy is capable of acquiring two distinct imaging modalities: the backscattering mode (BS-mode) and the photoacoustic mode (PA-mode). The BS-mode corresponds to the measurement of the static reflectivity of the sample. When either the excitation beam or the probe beam is incident in the PARS microscopy, the photodetector (PD) captures the light intensity reflected from the sample. A simplified schematic of the BS-mode configuration is shown in Fig. 1(a). On the other hand, the PA-mode corresponds to the measurement of the optical absorption within the sample. When both the excitation and probe beams are incident simultaneously, the sample absorbs the pulsed excitation light and generates an initial photoacoustic pressure, which induces a transient refractive index perturbation. This perturbation modulates the reflected probe intensity, and the resulting variations, captured by the PD, carry the photoacoustic information related to the local absorption within the sample. The schematic for PA-mode is presented in Fig. 1(b).
PSF of the BS-mode in PARS microscopy

In PARS microscopy, the BS-modes for the excitation and probe beams share a similar optical configuration. Therefore, this section derives the PSF of the BS-mode, taking the probe beam as a representative case.

Conceptual and equivalent optical modeling for PSF derivation. (a) Illustrative schematic of the relative spatial displacement between the sample and the focal point during the scanning process. (b) Equivalent optical configuration of the reflected probe path in the BS-mode.

As illustrated in Fig. 2(a), a coordinate system is established with the origin O at the focal point of the OL, and the z-axis aligned with the optical axis. The static reflectivity of the sample is denoted as . Consider an arbitrary point S within the sample, defined by coordinates . When the focal point of the PARS microscopy scans to S, the origin O of the coordinate system coincides with S. Consequently, the static reflectivity of the sample is rewritten as . Accordingly, the intensity distribution of the reflected probe beam in the object space is expressed as follows:
Here,  represents the amplitude PSF of the OL at the probe wavelength.

Fig. 2(b) presents the equivalent optical path for the reflected probe beam in the BS-mode. Specifically, the reflected beam is collected by the OL and focused by a tube lens onto the PD located at the focal plane of the image space. Since an infinity-corrected OL is employed, the reflected beam emerging from the OL is approximately collimated. In this case, the OL and the tube lens function equivalently as a single lens with an effective focal length .

Based on this simplified model, the intensity distribution of the sample at the image plane is the superposition of the intensities originating from each point within the object space, expressed as:where is the magnification factor of the equivalent optical path, and  is the amplitude PSF of the reflection path for the probe beam, with the expression:where  denotes the effective aperture function, determined by the aperture function of the OL. Since the focus is exclusively on the signal received by the PD at the image plane, the position vector of the image plane can be represented as . The resulting intensity distribution at the image plane is:

Based on Eq. (4), to enhance the spatial resolution of PARS microscopy, a pinhole aperture with an aperture function  can be placed at the image plane, following the principle of confocal microscopy. The intensity distribution at the image plane is then modified as:
Furthermore, the signal detected by the PD is the spatial integration of the light intensity at each position of the image plane, expressed as:
By exchanging the order of integration in Eq. (6), the expression becomes:
Considering that optical components such as the OL and the pinhole are symmetric about the optical axis, , , andare even functions. By defining , the following expression is obtained:

For an infinitely small point object, can be considered an impulse function. In this case, represents the intensity PSF of the BS-mode of the probe beam in PARS microscopy under pinhole confinement, denoted :
When pinhole confinement is not applied, that is, when , the contribution of the reflected component of the probe beam vanishes, and is determined solely by :

According to Eq. (10), and considering that the BS-mode of the excitation beam in PARS microscopy typically does not incorporate pinhole confinement, the corresponding intensity PSF  can be expressed as:where  denotes the amplitude PSF of the OL at the excitation wavelength.
Effective PSF of the PA-mode in PARS microscopy

The analysis of the effective PSF of the PA-mode in PARS microscopy requires accounting for the modulation of the reflected probe beam, which arises from refractive index variations induced by photoacoustic pressure through the elasto-optic effect. Specifically, when the excitation and probe beams are co-focused within the sample, the absorption of the excitation light generates an initial photoacoustic pressure  as follows:where  is the Grüneisen parameter,  represents the photothermal conversion efficiency, and denotes the optical absorption coefficient of the sample.  represents the optical fluence distribution of the excitation beam, which can be expressed by the amplitude PSF of the OL at the excitation wavelength, . Accordingly,  can be written as:

During the elasto-optic process,  leads to a refractive index change of the sample, denoted as , which is given by:where  is the refractive index of the sample,  represents the elasto-optic coefficient,  is the density, and  is the speed of sound in the medium. Considering the relationship between the refractive index and reflectivity, and assuming the refractive index of the surrounding medium is , the corresponding change in reflectivity of the sample, , is given by:
Following a first-order Taylor expansion, Eq. (15) can be rewritten as:
By substituting Eq. (14) and Eq. (13) into Eq. (16), the expression for  further becomes:
To simplify the expression, all material-dependent parameters except  are grouped into .When the focal point scans to position S,  is denoted as , and the reflectivity change  is written as:
Following the formulation in Eq. (7), the PARS signal intensity associated with the reflectivity change can be expressed as:
By applying the same transformation as in Eq. (8), the expression becomes:
Following the derivation in Eq. (9), the effective intensity PSF of the PA-mode in PARS microscopy, denoted as , is obtained as:
Eq. (21) indicates that the effective PSF of the PA-mode is jointly determined by both the excitation and probe BS-modes, corresponding to the product of their respective intensity PSFs.

Since the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PSF characterizes the spatial resolution of an optical microscopy system, the relationship between the resolutions of the BS-mode and the PA-mode can be derived from Eq. (21). Assuming that both  and  follow Gaussian spatial distributions, their product also follows a Gaussian distribution. Mathematically, the FWHM of  can be expressed in terms of the FWHMs of  and . Accordingly, the resolution of the PARS microscopy in the PA-mode, denoted as , is given by:where  and  represent the resolutions of the BS-mode of the excitation beam and the probe beam, respectively.
Experimental setup

Schematic diagram of the MC-PARS system. GM, galvanometer mirror; L, lens; HWP, half-wave plate; PBS, polarizing beam splitter; QWP, quarter-wave plate; BE, beam expander; M, mirror; DM, dichroic mirror; OL, objective lens; PD, photodetector; PH, pinhole.

To experimentally verify the derived PSF model, we developed a MC-PARS system, as illustrated in Fig. 3. The system was utilized to verify the resolution relationship described in Eq. (22), thereby further confirming the product relationship among , , and  as defined in Eq. (21).

The MC-PARS system comprises two excitation paths and two probe paths. The excitation beams are provided by pulsed lasers with a repetition rate of 100 kHz and a pulse width of 3 ns, operating at wavelengths of 1064 nm and 532 nm, respectively. Both excitation optical paths follow a similar configuration. The beams sequentially pass through half-wave plates (HWP1, HWP2), polarizing beam splitters (PBS1, PBS2), and quarter-wave plates (QWP1, QWP2) to be converted into circularly polarized beams. Subsequently, the excitation beams are expanded to a diameter of approximately 6 mm using beam expanders (BE1, BE2).

The probe beams are provided by superluminescent diodes (SLD1018P, SLD830S-A20, Thorlabs) at wavelengths of 830 nm and 1310 nm. Similar to the excitation paths, the two probe beams pass through HWPs (HWP3, HWP4), PBSs (PBS3, PBS4), QWPs (QWP3, QWP4), and BEs (BE3, BE4), resulting in circularly polarized beams with diameters of approximately 6 mm. The four beams are then combined via three dichroic mirrors (DM1, DM2, DM3, DMSP650, DMLP900, DMSP1180, Thorlabs) and are finally directed into the OL (LMM25X-P01, Thorlabs) by a two-dimensional galvanometer mirror scanner (GM, QS7XY-AG, Thorlabs), enabling multipath co-focusing within the sample.

The excitation and probe beams reflected from the sample are recollected by the OL and propagate backward along their respective optical paths. After passing through the corresponding QWPs, PBSs, and tube lenses, the beams are collected by photodetectors (PD1, PD2, PD3, PD4, DET10A2, DET10A2, APD130A2, Thorlabs, 1811FS, New Focus). Notably, interchangeable pinholes are introduced after the tube lens in the 830-nm probe paths to implement a confocal configuration. This design enables the modulation of  by employing pinholes with various diameters, thereby allowing experimental validation of the impact of pinhole aperture on .

Regarding the imaging process, 3D scanning of the sample is achieved by combining the GM with a 3D motorized stage (L509, PI). Detailed descriptions on the imaging procedures can be found in our previous work.
Results
PSF simulation and theoretical resolution

Numerical PSF simulation results and 1D intensity profiles based on the MC-PARS system configuration. White dashed lines indicate the positions of the extracted profiles. (a) Intensity PSF distributions of the BS-mode at 532 nm, 830 nm, 1064 nm, and 1310 nm. (b) 1D lateral intensity profiles corresponding to (a). (c) 1D axial intensity profiles corresponding to (a). (d) Intensity PSF distributions of the PA-mode under different wavelength combinations, where the values before and after / denote the excitation and probe wavelengths, respectively. (e) 1D lateral intensity profiles corresponding to (d). (f) 1D axial intensity profiles corresponding to (d).

Based on the MC-PARS system configuration, numerical simulations of , and were performed using the vector diffraction integral (see Supplementary S1). Fig. 4(a) shows the simulated lateral and axial intensity PSF distributions of the BS-mode at different wavelengths, and the corresponding one-dimensional (1D) profiles are presented in Figs. 4(b) and 4(c). Furthermore, based on the effective PSF model in Eq. (21), the  under different excitation-probe wavelength combinations was obtained as the product of the corresponding  and , as shown in Fig. 4(d). The arrangement in Fig. 4(d) follows that in Fig. 4(a), and the corresponding lateral and axial 1D intensity profiles are presented in Figs. 4(e) and 4(f), respectively.

A comparison between Figs. 4(a) and 4(d) reveals that the spot size  is consistently smaller than that of either  or , indicating that the PA-mode in PARS microscopy achieves a higher spatial resolution than the BS-mode. In addition, the variations in  under different wavelength combinations in Fig. 4(d) demonstrate that the effective PSF of the PA-mode is simultaneously determined by both the excitation and probe beam PSFs  and , rather than by either component alone.

The theoretical spatial resolutions for the BS-mode and PA-mode were determined by extracting the FWHM values from their respective 1D intensity profiles, as summarized in Table 1 and Table 2. To further examine the relationship described in Eq. (22), the BS-mode resolutions in Table 1 were substituted into Eq. (22) to yield the calculated PA-mode resolutions, which are also presented in Table 2. The strong agreement between the theoretical and the calculated results supports the validity of Eq. (22) from a numerical perspective. These results indicate that the spatial resolution of the PA-mode in PARS microscopy can be directly predicted from the BS-mode resolutions of the excitation and probe beams.
Impact of excitation and probe wavelengths on MC-PARS resolution

We performed resolution characterization experiments to further validate the proposed PSF model. Specifically, the resolution of the MC-PARS system was characterized by imaging a tungsten wire with approximately 6 μm diameter. We measured the spatial resolutions of the BS-mode and the PA-mode under various excitation-probe wavelength combinations. The measured resolutions were then substituted into Eq. (22) to verify its validity, thereby enabling experimental evaluation of the relationship among , , and  as described in Eq. (21).

Tungsten wire images and corresponding resolution characterization results obtained using the MC-PARS system with 532-nm excitation and 1310-nm probe beams. White dashed lines indicate the positions of the extracted profiles. (a) x-y plane images and lateral resolution characterization. (b) x-z plane images and axial resolution characterization.

Taking the 532-nm excitation and 1310-nm probe combination as an example, Fig. 5 presents the tungsten wire images and corresponding resolution characterization results under different imaging modes. The BS-mode signal acquisition is performed according to the configuration shown in Fig. 1(a). During measurement, either the excitation or probe beam is independently directed onto the sample, with the backscattered light captured by the PD. In Fig. 5(a), the first row shows the x-y plane images, and the second row presents the corresponding lateral resolution results. Similarly, the first row of Fig. 5(b) presents the x-z plane images, with the second row presenting the axial resolution results. As shown in Fig. 5, the PA-mode produces sharper and more distinct wire edges in the x-y plane. In the x-z plane, the PA-mode exhibits reduced background signal, indicating improved axial sectioning capability. Consistent with the theoretical simulations, these results demonstrate that both the lateral and axial resolutions of the PA-mode outperform those of the BS-mode.
Resolutions of the BS-mode and PA-mode in the MC-PARS system at different wavelengths.

passages.text: The spatial resolutions of all BS-modes and PA-modes under different wavelength combinations in the MC-PARS system were characterized using the same methodology as demonstrated in Fig. 5 (see Fig. S1 for results of the 1064-nm excitation and 830-nm probe combination). Each spatial resolution value was determined from five independent measurements (n = 5). By substituting the mean measured BS-mode resolutions into Eq. (22), we derived the calculated PA-mode resolutions, with all results summarized in Table 3. The comparison shows that, across all wavelength combinations, the ratio between the calculated and experimentally measured PA-mode resolutions ranges from 0.85 to 1.00, indicating good agreement between theoretical predictions and experimental findings. This provides further experimental evidence for the accuracy of the PSF model described in Eq. (21), confirming that the effective PSF of the PA-mode is jointly modulated by the excitation and probe BS-mode PSFs. Notably, the remaining discrepancies between the experimental and calculated values may be attributed to factors such as slight misalignment between the excitation and probe focal positions, and the influence of background noise.
Refinement of the effective PSF via confocal configuration

Following the validation of wavelength-dependent resolution, we further investigated the effect of the confocal configuration on the effective PSF of the MC-PARS system. Specifically, a fixed combination of 1064-nm excitation and 830-nm probe beams was used, and interchangeable pinholes with diameters of 10 μm, 20 μm, 35 μm, and 50 μm (PS1–10H/20H/35H/50H, LBTEK) were introduced into the probe path. We then measured the spatial resolutions of both the BS-mode and the PA-mode under these configurations. Notably, except for the 10-μm pinhole, all pinhole diameters exceeded the Airy disk size (∼13 μm) after collection by the OL and tube lens. As a result, the pinhole modulation is expected to be more pronounced in the axial direction than in the lateral direction (see Fig. S2). Accordingly, the following analysis focuses on the evolution of axial resolution.

Evolution of tungsten wire images and axial resolution of the PA-mode in the MC-PARS system (1064-nm excitation and 830-nm probe) as a function of pinhole size. White dashed lines indicate the positions of the extracted profiles. (a) x-z plane images of the tungsten wire without a pinhole and with 50-μm, 35-μm, 20-μm, and 10-μm pinholes. (b) Axial resolution characterization results for different pinhole diameters.

Figs. 6(a) and 6(b) present the x-z plane PA-mode images of the tungsten wire and the corresponding axial resolution characterization under different pinhole configurations. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the introduction of a pinhole suppresses out-of-focus photoacoustic signals and background noise, leading to reduced axial blurring and sharper wire edges. As the pinhole diameter decreases, the axial elongation in the images is gradually mitigated, indicating a progressive enhancement of the axial resolution and optical sectioning capability of the system. The quantitative results in Fig. 6(b) further support this trend. When the pinhole diameter is reduced to 10 μm, an axial resolution of 9.5 μm is achieved, representing a more than twofold improvement compared to the configuration without a pinhole. Concurrently, the image SNR remains above 45 dB (see Fig. S3), demonstrating that superior resolution is achieved without compromising essential image quality. Furthermore, to intuitively illustrate the impact of axial resolution enhancement on 3D imaging, we performed volumetric imaging of a crossed tungsten wire network using the MC-PARS system in both pinhole-free configuration and 10-μm pinhole configurations (see Fig. S4).
Axial resolutions of the BS-mode and PA-mode in the MC-PARS system under different pinhole sizes.

Furthermore, we compared the experimentally measured axial resolutions of the PA-mode with the calculated values based on Eq. (22) under different pinhole configurations, as summarized in Table 4 (n = 5). The results indicate that the pinhole function modulates, thereby improving the axial resolution of the probe BS-mode, and, consequently, that of the PA-mode. Across all pinhole sizes, the ratio between the calculated and the measured PA-mode resolutions ranges from 0.85 to 0.94, demonstrating excellent consistency. These results provide additional experimental support for the proposed PSF model in Eq. (21), demonstrating its high predictive accuracy across diverse system configurations and highlighting its robust adaptability.
Discussion

In this study, we established a theoretical PSF model for PARS microscopy, elucidating the joint contribution of excitation and probe beams. Using the developed MC-PARS system, we validated the effectiveness and adaptability of the model across a range of excitation-probe wavelength combinations. In addition, by integrating the confocal mechanism, we achieved effective modulation and contraction of the effective PSF. These findings provide a solid theoretical basis for understanding and optimizing the imaging performance, and for ultimately surpassing the resolution limits of PARS microscopy.

By analyzing the PSFs of the BS modes and establishing the coupling relationship between the excitation beam and the photoacoustic-sensitive probe beam, we successfully derived an effective PSF model for the PA-mode in PARS microscopy. The model clearly reveals that the final imaging resolution of PARS is jointly determined by the PSFs of both the excitation and probe beams. This dual-beam confocal scheme leads to a PSF formulation fundamentally distinct from that of conventional confocal microscopy, thereby explaining why the PA-mode in PARS microscopy typically achieves higher resolution than the individual BS modes. Building upon this unique PSF mechanism, the incorporation of a confocal detection configuration enables a theoretical resolution improvement by a factor of  in PARS microscopy, whereas confocal microscopy typically achieves the -fold enhancement relative to widefield imaging. This highlights the fundamentally different resolution scaling behavior between the two modalities. Moreover, the proposed model also highlights a fundamental distinction in resolution determinants between PARS and conventional OR-PAM. In OR-PAM, the acoustic focal spot of the transducer is typically significantly larger than the optical focus; thus, the lateral resolution is primarily governed by the excitation beam. In contrast, in PARS microscopy, the resolution scales of the excitation and probe beams are comparable (generally within the same order of magnitude). As a result, the collective influence of both beams should be considered to accurately predict system performance. This theoretical framework addresses a gap in the fundamental imaging theory of PARS microscopy and provides a basis for resolution optimization and system design.

The proposed PSF model (Eq. (21)) was validated through experiments under multiple system configurations. For different excitation-probe wavelength combinations, the theoretically predicted resolutions show excellent agreement with the experimentally measured values (Table 3, Fig. 5). This not only confirms the validity of the model but also demonstrates its applicability for predicting and optimizing system performance. Furthermore, by introducing a confocal pinhole to actively modulate the probe PSF, we achieved a twofold enhancement in the axial resolution of the MC-PARS system (Fig. 6, Fig. S4), while maintaining a high SNR above 45 dB (Fig. S4). The model maintained consistent predictive accuracy across different pinhole sizes (Table 4), demonstrating its reliability and versatility across various optical configurations.

The unique PSF composition in PARS microscopy provides considerable flexibility for system optimization. Specifically, the effective PSF can be refined by modifying either the coherent pulsed excitation path or the incoherent continuous-wave probe path. This characteristic provides distinct degrees of freedom for resolution enhancement. Notably, by performing PSF engineering on the probe path, resolution can be enhanced without the need for high-intensity excitation, thereby preventing potential photodamage while maintaining stable photoacoustic signal excitation. This approach complements existing OR-PAM strategies, such as nonlinear photoacoustic effects or complex excitation modulation. These findings provide a theoretical and experimental basis for integrating advanced PSF engineering strategies, including structured illumination, FED-like subtraction, and Fourier reweighting, into PARS microscopy. Ultimately, this work aligns PARS microscopy with the established PSF engineering paradigms of advanced optical microscopy, paving the way to circumvent the resolution constraints in non-contact photoacoustic imaging.
Conclusion

In this study, we systematically established and validated a theoretical PSF model for PARS microscopy, revealing that the system resolution is jointly determined by the PSFs of both the excitation and probe paths. Using the developed MC-PARS system, the proposed model was evaluated through numerical simulations and experimental validation. The results demonstrate that the model remains reliable and effective across diverse excitation-probe wavelength combinations and confocal pinhole configurations. This work not only provides a framework for PSF analysis in PARS microscopy but also demonstrates effective PSF contraction through active system-level design. Our work builds a solid foundation for integrating advanced PSF engineering into PARS microscopy, providing a clear pathway towards surpassing resolution limits in non-contact photoacoustic microscopy.
